coredata.dc:title: Lipid Droplet-Targeting near-infrared carbon dots with High-Specificity Dual-Report function for diagnosing hepatocellular carcinoma and evaluating its pharmacodynamics
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Junli
Sun, Yuanqiang
Hu, Jingyu
Guo, Yifei
Qu, Lingbo
Li, Zhaohui
coredata.dc:description: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is a metabolic disease with high mortality caused by abnormal deposition of lipid droplets (LDs). The number and distribution of LDs can effectively reflect the severity of HCC. Additionally, polarity change is also an important indicator for monitoring HCC. Therefore, using fluorescence imaging technology to accurately identify LDs is helpful for HCC diagnosis and treatment evaluation. To date, the study that can simultaneously monitor the polarity and spatiotemporal characteristics of LDs to diagnose HCC has not been reported yet. Herein, a novel LD-targeting near-infrared carbon dots (CDs) with dual-report function of determining the LDs polarity as well as presenting their spatial characteristics was developed. The high lipophilicity and solvatochromic effect of CDs endows it with the ability to image LDs with high-fidelity, so it can not only report the spatiotemporal characteristics of LDs including the number, size, and aggregation degree, but also determine their polarity variations. Therefore, the high-contrast fluorescence diagnosis of HCC was successfully realized by using dual-function CDs. Meanwhile, the CDs were further utilized to evaluate the efficacy of anticancer drugs with satisfactory performance. Overall, this CD-based LDs probe may provide a new perspective for the diagnosing of LDs-related diseases and screening of anticancer drugs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Lipid droplets
Polarity
Hepatocellular carcinoma
Pharmacodynamic

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Rational design and Characterization of the CDs
Ultrasensitive response of CDs towards polarity
Determine the polarity and monitor the spatial characteristics of LDs to achieve HCC diagnosis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization and optical properties of CDs. (a) The TEM image of CDs, inset: corresponding size distribution histogram. (b) High resolution TEM image of CDs. (c) XRD pattern of CDs. (d) The FT-IR spectra, (e) The full XPS spectra, and (f) The high-resolution XPS spectra of CDs. (g) The fluorescence spectra of the CDs (5 μg mL−1) in the DIOX/H2O mixtures with different water fractions (fw). (h) Linearity between the maximum fluorescence intensity or emission wavelength and the polarity parameter Δf (Δf = 0.020–0.229). (i) The fluorescence spectra of the CDs (5 μg mL−1) in PBS buffer and n-octanol (λex = 535 nm).

Research on wash-free cell imaging experiment. (a-f) Confocal fluorescence images of living SMMC-7721 cells stained with CDs (5 μg mL−1) with or without washing steps. (g) Relative FL intensity was obtained through panels (a) and (c). Scale bar: 20 µm.

Cell colocalization experiment. (a-b) Colocalization images of living SMMC-7721 cells with HCS LipidTOXTM Deep Red (1:1000 dilution, green pseudocolor) subsequently with the CDs (5 μg mL−1, red pseudocolor). (c) The merged images of channels (a) and (b). (d) The scatter plot represents a correction coefficient of (a) and (b). (e) The spatial fluorescence intensity distribution of the white line in (c) image. (f) 3D image of the CDs and Hoechst 33342. (a1) and (b1) are the enlarged images from the area marked by (a) and (b), respectively. Scale bar: 20 μm.

Cell imaging of HCC cells and non-HCC cells. (a) Confocal images of living HCC cells (Huh-7, SMMC-7721, and HepG2 cells), living normal cells (HL-7702 cells) and other types of cancer cells (HeLa, MCF-7 cells) treated with the CDs (5 μg mL−1), and differences in the degree of accumulation of LDs in HCC cells and normal cells. (b) In-situ normalized FL emission spectra. (c) Linearity between the maximum fluorescence intensity or emission wavelength and the polarity parameter Δf of a1-a4. (d) the number, and (e) size of LDs from (a1-a4). (f) The changes of fluorescence intensity from (a1-a4). Scale bar: 20 μm.

The efficacy evaluation experiment. (a) Schematic diagram of LDs metabolic changes in HCC cells before and after sorafenib treatment. (b) Confocal images of living SMMC-7721 cells treated with 0, 2, 5, 10, 20, and 30 μM sorafenib for 24 h, and then stained with 5 μg mL−1 CDs. (c) Fluorescence intensity, (d) LDs numbers, and (e) LDs size change with incubation concentration. (f) In-situ normalized FL emission spectra. Scale bar: 20 μm.
(a) Schematic diagram of synthetic CDs. (b) Schematic representation nD-π-nA structures. (c) Application of dual-report function for LD-targeting CDs.

head.ce:title: Lipid Droplet-Targeting near-infrared carbon dots with High-Specificity Dual-Report function for diagnosing hepatocellular carcinoma and evaluating its pharmacodynamics

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Institute of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Green Construction of Functional Molecules and Their Bioanalytical Applications, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is a metabolic disease with high mortality caused by abnormal deposition of lipid droplets (LDs). The number and distribution of LDs can effectively reflect the severity of HCC. Additionally, polarity change is also an important indicator for monitoring HCC. Therefore, using fluorescence imaging technology to accurately identify LDs is helpful for HCC diagnosis and treatment evaluation. To date, the study that can simultaneously monitor the polarity and spatiotemporal characteristics of LDs to diagnose HCC has not been reported yet. Herein, a novel LD-targeting near-infrared carbon dots (CDs) with dual-report function of determining the LDs polarity as well as presenting their spatial characteristics was developed. The high lipophilicity and solvatochromic effect of CDs endows it with the ability to image LDs with high-fidelity, so it can not only report the spatiotemporal characteristics of LDs including the number, size, and aggregation degree, but also determine their polarity variations. Therefore, the high-contrast fluorescence diagnosis of HCC was successfully realized by using dual-function CDs. Meanwhile, the CDs were further utilized to evaluate the efficacy of anticancer drugs with satisfactory performance. Overall, this CD-based LDs probe may provide a new perspective for the diagnosing of LDs-related diseases and screening of anticancer drugs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) is the sixth most common cancer worldwide, with more than 750,000 new cases each year, and its mortality rate ranks the fourth in the world [1–4]. The development of an effective method is still eagerly needed to accurately diagnose the HCC and evaluate the therapeutic effect of anticancer drugs [5–7]. The occurrence of HCC causes great changes in the normal lipids metabolism [8]. Lipid droplets (LDs) are the storage centers of intracellular lipids, and the LDs deposition caused by lipid metabolism disorder is the main reason for the occurrence and development of HCC [9–12]. In the early stage of hepatocellular carcinoma, due to the up-regulation of fatty acid intake/synthesis and down-regulation of oxidation, excessive lipid in hepatocytes accumulates abnormally in the form of LDs [10,11], which will trigger the rapid reaction of LDs and change the number and spatial state of LDs [13,14]. Thus the distribution and quantity of LDs reflect the severity of HCC, LDs can be used as an important indicator for identifying HCC.

Apart from spatiotemporal characteristics of LDs, the microenvironment of HCC has also changed significantly. Among them, polarity has drawn much attention because it plays an important role in many metabolic reactions and determines the biological functions of HCC, such as enzyme-based catalysis and functional proteins activation [15–18]. Accumulating evidence suggests that the abnormal fluctuation of the polarity is inseparable from metabolic diseases, especially HCC [15,19,20]. Moreover, the polarity changes are reversible along with the development and prognosis of the diseases [21,22]. Consequently, polarity can be used as another indicator for diagnosis of HCC and evaluation of its pharmacodynamics. Unfortunately, there is still a lack of reports that can simultaneously monitor the polarity fluctuation of LDs and visualize the spatial characteristics of LDs.

Near-infrared (NIR) fluorescence imaging is a robust and reliable technology for cancer diagnosis and subcellular organelle microenvironment detection with the merit of high sensitivity, real-time monitoring, and non-invasive in situ imaging [23–27]. At present, many fluorescent probes based on intramolecular charge transfer effect (ICT) use fluorescence imaging technology to monitor the polarity of living cells. Among them, carbon dots (CDs), as a fluorescent nanomaterial with excellent properties, have been widely used for polarity imaging of various organelles and cancer diagnosis [28–32]. To date, although a variety of LD-responsive CDs have been developed, these probes cannot specifically diagnose HCC based on the spatiotemporal characteristics and polarity fluctuations of LDs. Therefore, the design of multifunctional CDs that simultaneously monitor the polarity and spatiotemporal characteristics of LDs can effectively realize the diagnosis of HCC.

In view of the above situation, we have developed a high-fidelity NIR LD-targeting CDs with polarity-ultrasensitive response characteristics, which can realize high-contrast fluorescence diagnosis of HCC and evaluate the pharmacodynamics of anti-liver cancer drugs (Scheme 1 ). The fluorescent CDs with high lipophilicity (Log D = 1.65) and enhanced ICT effect can specifically light up LDs and monitor the polarity, which are two indicators of the macroscopic and microscopic changes in the development of HCC. The CDs with dual-report function not only provides the difference in the spatiotemporal characteristics (number, size and aggregation state) of LDs between cells, but also distinguishes the LDs polarity of different cells, which makes the HCC be recognized successfully. Finally, the CDs successfully evaluated the potential of anticancer drugs (sorafenib) in terms of efficacy, and provided an important LDs change model for drug development and efficacy evaluation.

N, N-Diethyl-p-phenylenediamine (98%) was obtained from Energy Chemical (Shanghai, China). HCS LipidTOXTM Deep Red and Hoechst 33,342 were supplied by Thermo Fisher Scientific (Pittsburgh, USA), respectively. 3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2-H-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the chemical reagents used in the experiment were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Endocytosis inhibitors including chlorpromazine (CPZ), methyl-β-cyclodextrin (MβCD), amiloride (AMI), genistein, sulfobromophthalein (BSP) were bought from Sigma Aldrich (MO, USA).

The LD-targeting CDs were successfully prepared via a simple one-pot solvothermal method. Trivially, n, n-diethyl-p-phenylene-diamine (150 mg) was added in absolute ethanol (15 mL). Subsequently, the resulting mixed solution was put into a 50 mL teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The obtained crude products were further purified by silica gel column chromatography with ethyl acetate-petroleum gradient elution.

The MTT method was employed to evaluate the cytotoxicity of CDs to SMMC-7721 cells. At first, SMMC-7721 cells were seeded into 96-well plates and allowed to adhere at 37 °C and 5% CO2. After 24 h, the old culture medium was removed and replaced with 100 μL of fresh culture medium with the CDs (at 0, 1, 2, 5, 10, and 20 μg mL−1), incubating for an additional 24 h. 5 mg mL−1 MTT solution was subsequently added to the 96-well plates at 10 μL/well for 4 h. Next, the supernatant was removed carefully and 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to each well to dissolve the formazan crystals for 10 min at 37 °C. Finally, absorbance values at 570 nm were measured with a microplate reader. Cell viability was expressed as the percentage of the absorbance values of CD-treated cells to that of untreated cells.

All kinds of cells used in the experiment were grown in Dulbecco modified Eagle medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum and 1% (v/v) penicillin–streptomycin under a 5% CO2 and 37 °C. The cells were seeded on a glass-bottom cells culture dish (ϕ20 mm) and cultured for 24 h until becoming adherent. The cells were first washed once with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) buffer before imaging. Subsequently, the cells were treated with Hoechst 33,342 (1 μL) for 20 min in the dark and then washed with PBS. Finally, the cells were incubated with 1 mL DMEM containing 5 μg mL−1 CDs for 1 min and then directly imaged using a Leica TCS SP8 confocal microscope with 63x oil immersion objective. (λex = 496 nm, λem = 570–620 nm).

For the LDs co-localization experiment, the SMMC-7721 cells were planted in a 20 mm-glass bottom dish and cultured for 24 h. Next, the SMMC-7721 cells were first pre-treated with HCS LipidTOXTM Deep Red (1:1000 dilution) for 15 min in the dark and then washed with PBS. Afterward, the SMMC-7721 cells were incubated with the CDs (5 μg mL−1) for 1 min and then directly imaged. (CDs: λex = 496 nm, λem = 570–620 nm; HCS LipidTOXTM Deep Red: λex = 630 nm, λem = 640–680 nm).

The SMMC-7721 cells were grown in glass-bottom dishes for 24 h before carrying out an internalization mechanism study of CDs. For the energy-dependent experiment, the SMMC-7721 cells were first pre-treated at 4 °C for 2 h and then incubated with Hoechst 33,342 (1 μL) in the dark. After 20 min, the cells were washed three times with PBS, followed by being incubated with 5 μg mL−1 CDs. Correspondingly, five energy-dependent endocytosis inhibitors were selected for studying the endocytosis pathway of CDs. The cells were treated with chlorpromazine (CPZ) (5 μg mL−1, 2 h), β-cyclodextrin (MβCD) (5 μg mL−1, 2 h), amiloride (AMI) (10 μg mL−1, 2 h), genistein (50 μg mL−1, 2 h), and sulfobromophthalein (BSP) (250 μM, 10 min), and then washed three times with PBS. Afterward, Hoechst 33,342 (1 μL) was added and incubated in the dark for 20 min. After washing three times with PBS, further incubated with 5 μg mL−1 CDs, and imaged by confocal microscopy. (CPZ, an inhibitor of clathrin-mediated endocytosis, MβCD, an inhibitor of lipid raft-mediated endocytosis, AMI, an inhibitor of macropinocytosis, genistein, an inhibitor of caveolae-mediated endocytosis, and BSP, a competitive inhibitor of OATP).

All quantitative data are based on at least triple independent experiments (n ≥ 3) and the data are expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD). Fluorescence analyses of cell were performed by Leica SP8 CLSM. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used to evaluate the statistical analysis using OriginPro 2021. The differences were considered significant if p < 0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rational design and Characterization of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Since LDs are the most hydrophobic of all organelles, lipophilic probes are more likely to interact with LDs [30]. Therefore, N, N-diethyl-p-phenylenediamine with suitable lipophilicity (Clog P = 2.14, determined by ChemDraw) was selected as the CDs precursor and treated by solvothermal method (Scheme 1a). In addition, the surface and the carbon core of the CDs are full of functional groups, such as electron-withdrawing functional groups -C = O, -C = N in carbon core or surface could serve as the electron acceptor, while the electron-donating –NH2 or –OH functional groups could serve as the electron donor, which can form a powerful ICT with numerous donor-π-acceptor (nD-π-nA) in CDs. The strong electron donating ability of diethylamino enhances the ICT, making the energy of the ICT state is strongly dependent on the polarity-induced solvation relaxation and structural changes, which means that the CDs have higher sensitivity toward polarity (Scheme 1b). Therefore, the CDs with the ability of high-fidelity imaging of LDs and determining their polarity has been successfully developed (Scheme 1c).

Next, a series of characterizations of CDs were carried out. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) proved that we have successfully prepared spherical CDs with an average particle size of 2.5 ± 0.9 nm (Fig. 1 a). Besides, the high-resolution TEM shows that the CDs have lattice fringe with a spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 1b), belonging to the (100) facet of graphene. This result is further verified by the wide peak of about 2θ = 24° shown by X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. 1c). The multiple functional groups of the CDs were characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), respectively. As can be seen from FT-IR (Fig. 1d), the stretching vibration peak of the -O-H/N-H group appeared at 3440 cm−1 [33,34]. The peak at 1730 cm−1 was due to stretching vibrations of -C = O/C = N. The peaks at 1384, 1213, and 1102 cm−1 attributed to -N-H bending vibration, -C-O stretching vibration, and -C-N stretching vibration, respectively [35]. Furthermore, XPS spectra also confirmed the existence of electron donating –OH and -NR2 groups, as well as the electron withdrawing -C = O, -C = N and pyridinic N (Fig. 1e, f) [36,37]. As shown in Fig. S1a, the CDs exhibit significant absorption bands at 562 nm, which may be related to the n-π* transition of surface functional groups. In addition, CDs show maximum emission at 688 nm, with a Stokes shift of up to 126 nm, which is a typical feature of ICT [14,38]. Furthermore, the fluorescence spectrum indicated that the CDs exhibited the excitation-independent emission feature (Fig. S1b). And compared with the photostability of commercial BODIPY 493/503 and Nile Red, CDs have excellent photostability (Fig. S1c). In conclusion, the as-prepared CDs with multiple electron donors and acceptors could serve as the basis to form the nD-π-nA structures for significantly improving ICT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ultrasensitive response of CDs towards polarity
ce:section.ce:para: To prove that the synthesized CDs have excellent polarity response-ability, firstly, the solvatochromism of CDs was evaluated by studying its emission spectra in the solvents with different polarities. With the solvent polarity increasing, an obvious decrease in the emission intensity is observed and a corresponding significant red shift in the emission wavelength with is also observed (Fig. S2a). The emission wavelength has a good linear relationship with the solvent polarity parameter (ET(30)), indicating that CDs have a solvatochromism effect, which is attributed to the ICT effect. Next, a dioxane/H2O mixed system with different water content (fw) was used to tune the changes in polarity and further test the polarity response ability of CDs [39,40]. The maximum emission wavelength of the sample displayed red-shift from 600 nm (dioxane) to 688 nm (H2O) (Fig. S2b), with a 505-fold of decrease in the maximum fluorescence intensity. Even in the presence of as low as 1% water fraction, the emission wavelength could lead to 14 nm red-shifted emission band with an obvious emission decrease (Fig. 1g and Fig. S3). In addition, the fluorescence intensity and emission wavelength were established an excellent linear relationship with Δf within the range of Δf = 0.020 (0% H2O) to 0.229 (10% H2O), respectively (Fig. 1h). As outlined above, the CDs showed high sensitivity to tiny polarity changes.

In general, n-octanol is employed to simulate the lipid environment in vitro [41]. As shown in Fig. 1i, the CDs could emit a strong red fluorescence in n-octanol, and its fluorescence intensity was enhanced almost 254-fold in comparison with that of in PBS buffer. The quantum yield was drastically improved from 0.2% (PBS) to 13.8% (n-octanol) (Table S1). The measured Log D value of the CDs was 1.65 via a shake flask-ultraviolet spectrophotometric method (Fig. S4) [30], indicating the high hydrophobicity of the CDs. Furthermore, the CDs owned some advantages, including robust anti-interference ability, high pH stability, good biocompatibility, and excellent photostability (Fig. S5–S8). In summary, the polarity ultra-sensitivity of CDs would hold great potential in serving as a targeting probe for LDs.

The cell permeability and optimal incubation concentration of the CDs were first investigated. The CDs (5 μg mL−1) could penetrate through the cell membrane quickly and the intracellular fluorescence intensity rapidly reached a steady state within 1 min (Fig. S9). Interestingly, CDs can remain in LDs for a long time without being internalized (at least 12 h) and still maintain high-quality imaging effects (Fig. S10), demonstrating CDs have the ability to serve as fluorescence imaging tools for long-term biological observation. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity and background signal of living cells before and after washing were almost unchanged (Fig. 2 ), suggesting that the CDs have good potential in wash-free imaging [42–45]. These results were in good agreement with those in vitro (Fig. 1h).

To prove that the CDs were specifically located in LDs, a co-localization experiment of the CDs with the commercially available staining probe for LDs, HCS LipidTOXTM Deep Red, was carried out in living SMMC-7721 cells. No cross-talk between the CDs channel and the LD-probe channel was observed (Fig. S11). After co-incubation, the red fluorescence of the CDs channel overlapped well with the green fluorescence of the commercial HCS LipidTOXTM Deep Red channel (Fig. 3 a–c). In addition, the Pearson’s correlation coefficient was determined to be 0.95 (Fig. 3d). The spatial fluorescence intensity distribution was also consistent (Fig. 3e). Moreover, the CDs exhibited a higher S/N of 12.1-fold in comparison to the commercial LD-probe (7.1-fold) (Fig. 3a1-b1). These results suggested that the CDs can achieve high-quality fluorescence imaging of LDs. The internalization mechanism study of the CDs demonstrated that the CDs were internalized into cells through the synergistic effect of caveola-mediated endocytosis and organic-anion transporting polypeptide (OATP) transporters-mediated endocytosis (Fig. S12). Furthermore, a three-dimensional (3D) confocal fluorescence image of the CDs showed that the CDs could be used to visualize the spatial distribution of LDs in living cells (Fig. 3f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determine the polarity and monitor the spatial characteristics of LDs to achieve HCC diagnosis
ce:section.ce:para: A large amount of lipid accumulation can lead to changes in the LDs polarity. Therefore, since CDs exhibit ultra-high sensitivity to polarity and high selectivity to LDs, we next investigate whether CDs have the potential to quantify the LDs polarity in living cells and realize the diagnosis of HCC. To this end, three HCC cell lines (Huh-7, SMMC-7721, and HepG2 cells) and one normal hepatocyte line (HL-7702 cells) were selected as the research model for this experiment. The strong red fluorescence intensities were observed in the three types of HCC cell lines, while the normal hepatocyte line exhibited weak fluorescence emission (Fig. 4 a), indicating that the polarity of LDs in liver cancer cells was much lower than that in normal liver cells. The in-situ emission spectrum also proves this view. From the in-situ emission spectrum data, it can be seen that the emission peaks of HCC cells were located in the range of 600–602 nm, while the normal hepatocytes exhibited the main emission peak at 611 nm, (Fig. 4b and Fig. S13). Moreover, the Δf of different cells was obtained from the linear regression equation in Fig. 1h. The Δf of HCC cells was in the range of 0.021–0.032, in comparison, the Δf of normal hepatocytes was 0.081 (Fig. 4c). Therefore, the CDs successfully determines the polarity of LDs in normal cells and cancer cells.

In addition to monitoring the LDs polarity the CDs also successfully reported the differences in the spatial characteristics of LDs between cells. It can be observed intuitively that the distribution of LDs in cancer cells is closer than that in normal cells, that is, the aggregation degree of LDs in cancer cells is higher (Fig. 4a1–a4). The statistical results show that the number and size of LDs in HCC cells are significantly higher than those in hepatocytes as well as more robust fluorescence intensity of the single LD in HCC (Fig. 4d, e). In addition, fluorescence statistics indicated that the average fluorescence intensity of each cell of the HCC cell lines was more than 3.8-fold higher than that of hepatocytes (Fig. 4f), which successfully realizes the fluorescence high-contrast diagnosis of HCC. These results proved that the CDs could distinguish the tiny polarity difference of LDs in HCC and normal hepatocytes (Table S2). In other word, HCC cells could be easily distinguished from the normal hepatocytes by using the CDs as the imaging reagent.

More interestingly, the CDs were successfully used for distinguishing HCC from other cancer cells (HeLa and MCF-7 cells) through quantitative monitoring of LDs polarity. Robust red fluorescence was observed in HCC cells, while no obvious fluorescence exhibited in HeLa and MCF-7 cells (Fig. 4a5, a6). The fluorescence intensities of HCC cells were 3.7-fold higher than those of other cancer cells (Fig. S14a). In situ emission spectra (Fig. S14b–S15) showed that the emission peaks of cancer cells were mainly between 608 and 609 nm, and the corresponding Δf range was 0.065–0.070 (Table S2). The numbers and sizes of LDs in HCC cells were higher than those in other cancer cells (Fig. S14c, d). These fluorescent differences were attributed to the fact that HCC cells have the lower polarity than other cancer cells. Overall, the CDs with ultra-sensitive polarity response could quantitatively monitor the LDs polarity in different cells, and report the difference in the spatial characteristics of LDs to achieve high-contrast fluorescence diagnosis of HCC.

A reliable experimental method was established for evaluating the efficiency of the anti-HCC drugs by employing LDs as a novel biomarker (Fig. 5 a). Currently, sorafenib, the only anti-HCC approved for the treatment of HCC, was employed as the model drug [46,47]. First, the drug concentration-dependent therapeutic effects were investigated by monitoring the fluctuation of LDs using the CDs as the imaging reagent (Fig. 5b). The fluorescence of the LDs decreased sharply with the enhancement of the therapeutic effects of sorafenib (Fig. 5c). Such a huge fluorescence decrease indicated that the spatiotemporal characteristics and polarity of LDs have changed with the increase of treatment effectiveness. Next, the potential of CDs in evaluating the efficacy of anticancer drugs (sorafenib) is evaluated by observing the spatiotemporal characteristics of LDs, including the number, size, and aggregation state of LDs. Interestingly, the aggregate state (Fig. 5b), the average number (Fig. 5d) and the size (Fig. 5e) of the LDs decreased sharply with an increase in the concentration of sorafenib. More significantly, we found that with the treatment of HCC, the polarity of LDs increased, that is, the polarity of LDs was 0.021 (λem = 600 nm) in untreated HCC, while after treatment with sorafenib, the LDs polarity increased to 0.103 (λem = 615 nm) (taking 20 μm sorafenib treatment as an example) (Fig. 5f, Fig. S16 and Table S2), which confirmed that treatment can lead to reversible changes in the LDs polarity. Moreover, the time-dependent therapy effect of sorafenib was examined. The fluorescence intensity, the aggregate state, the average number, and the size of the LDs gradually reduced with the extension of incubation time (Fig. S17 and Table S2). These interesting results demonstrated that CDs can provide an important LDs change model for drug development and drug efficacy evaluation by accurately monitoring the changes of LDs during the treatment of anti-cancer drugs.

Collectively, a LD-specific NIR polarity-ultrasensitive fluorescent CDs was developed through a facile one-step solvothermal method. The CDs have a large Stokes shift of 126 nm, high brightness (QY = 71.4%), and excellent biocompatibility and photostability. Furthermore, due to its high lipophilicity and strong solvatochromic effect, the CDs can specifically image LDs with high-fidelity, giving the CDs the dual-report function of determining the polarity of LDs and presenting the spatiotemporal characteristics of LDs. The fluorescence high-contrast diagnosis of HCC was realized by analyzing the difference of LDs polarity and the spatiotemporal characteristics of the number, size and aggregation of LDs among different cells. Meanwhile, the CDs also successfully monitor the changes of LDs treated with anti-liver cancer drugs (sorafenib), making it used for the evaluation of HCC drug treatment, and proving that changes in LDs polarity are reversible. Altogether, this excellent LDs probe for diagnosing HCC not only provides accurate information for the diagnosis and resection of HCC, as well as for the screening of future anti-liver cancer drugs, but also has the potential to be used for monitoring of the therapeutic effects of anti-liver cancer drugs in vivo.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22274143 and 21974125), Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in University of Henan Province (22TRTSTHN002), Projects of the Joint Fund for Science and Technology of Henan Province (222301420007), and Excellent Youth Foundation of Henan Scientific Committee (232300421021).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.145762.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Comprehensive metabolic profiling and genome-wide analysis reveal therapeutic modalities for hepatocellular carcinoma

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Qi, J. Li, Z. Qi, J. Zhang, B. Zhou, B. Yang, W. Qin, W. Cui, J. Xia, Comprehensive Metabolic Profiling and Genome-wide Analysis Reveal Therapeutic Modalities for Hepatocellular Carcinoma, Research. 6 (2023) 0036.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Update in global trends and aetiology of hepatocellular carcinoma

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Rawla, T. Sunkara, P. Muralidharan, J.P. Raj, Update in global trends and aetiology of hepatocellular carcinoma, Contemp. Oncol. 22 (2018) 141-150.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Immune-based therapy clinical trials in hepatocellular carcinoma

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Liu, K.F. Staveley-O, G. Li, Immune-based Therapy Clinical Trials in Hepatocellular Carcinoma, J. Clin. Cell Immunol. 6 (2015) 1000376.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synergistic sonodynamic/chemotherapeutic suppression of hepatocellular carcinoma by targeted biodegradable mesoporous nanosonosensitizers

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Characterization of lipid and lipid droplet metabolism in human HCC
